2: In Europe, intensive livestock breeding and subsequent land application of animal manure led to excessive fertilization of agri- cultural areas, with environmental problems as a consequence. These problems include the runoff of excessively applied nitrate and phosphate to surface waters, but also the atmospheric emis- sions of NH3, NO and N20. The runoff problems are mainly caused by inaccurate application, not taking spatial and temporal varia- tions in soil fertility and plant uptake of nutrients into account, leading to a low nutrient efficiency (Stoate et al., 2001).

For this reason, the EU council, parliament and commission implemented regulations and directives, of which the nitrates directive (91/676/EEC) is the most important. In this directive limits were set for the application rate of livestock manure, expressed as the amount of nitrogen per hectare of land.
The growing concern about environmental consequences of excessive fertilization necessitates alternative treatment options of

animal manure, such as refeeding to animals, composting, biogas production or combustion (Edwards and Daniel, 1992; Bolan et al., 2010; Hamelin et al., 2011; Kelleher et al., 2002). Full scale poultry manure combustors are operational in e.g. the UK, the USA and the Netherlands. The primary function of the combustors is the pro- duction of electricity, the ash being recycled as a soil conditioner. In this paper, the installation of BMC Moerdijk (NL) will be described, a fluidized bed combustor fed with poultry manure. Although other promising treatment techniques exist, this paper focuses on assessing the environmental performance of poultry manure combustion, with the most common alternative, land spreading, as a benchmark.

Organic carbon in poultry manure, i.e. digested animal feed, is of biogenic origin. CO2 formed by oxidation of biogenic carbon is carbon neutral and should not be taken into account for when the impact on global warming is assessed. Moreover, combustion of by- products from biomass production, such as poultry manure, gen- erates renewable energy, replacing fossil fuel combustion. This way it contributes to achieving the targets for renewable energy set by Directive 2009/28/EC of the EU parliament and council.

In this paper it will be shown that combustion of poultry manure, with subsequent ash recycling, has two major advantages over its land application: (i) the energy content of the biomass is valorized as carbon neutral and renewable energy, and (ii) valuable

6: The studied electric power plant consists of a bubbling fluidized bed combustor, followed by an energy recovery section and an intensive flue gas cleaning installation, including an electrostatic precipitator (ESP), a semidry scrubber with baghouse filter (Turbosorp and a selective catalytic reductor (SCR) (Fig. 1). The bubbling fluidized bed operates at a temperature of 750-765 °C. 9300 Mg year of silica sand is used as fresh bed material. In the post-combustion zone (freeboard), secondary air is injected and the temperature rises to above 900 °C. The ESP removes 97% of the dust from the flue gas. In the Turbosorp reactor, lime (Ca(OH)2) is added to react with the acid gasses (HCI, SO2) in the flue gas. In the SCR, NO is converted into N2-gas by catalytic reduction with added NH3 (200 Mg year of a 25% ammonia solution), after reheating the flue gas to 195 °C by combustion of natural gas (1100 Mg year but this has been decreased recently).

The combusted poultry manure has a heating value between 6 and 8 MJ/kg. The capacity of the combustion plant is 31 MW net, with a net electricity yield of approximately 28% (i.e. the net effi- ciency). The efficiency is lower than that of comparable coal plants, because the sticky nature of the ash and corrosiveness of the flue gas limit the steam temperature in the boiler. As a consequence, the emissions of poultry manure combustion will be higher per kWh of electricity produced.

The throughput of the installation is 440,000 Mg year of poultry manure, originating from more than 600 chicken farms all over the Netherlands. Throughout this paper, the term manure will be used to cover the poultry droppings mixed with bedding ma- terial (straw, sawdust, etc.) and feathers, as it leaves the shed. The average composition is given in Table 1. It was shown in literature that the ammoniacal nitrogen (NH3-N) can range up to 3 wt% of the wet manure, while the manure contains <0.15 wt% of nitrate nitrogen (NO3-N) (Edwards and Daniel, 1992).

Although the energy production from poultry manure is a proven technology, the major remaining challenge is related to the nature of the ash. The high phosphorus and potassium concentra- tion in the ash are responsible for agglomeration in the bed, for the formation of deposits in the freeboard and for fouling of the boiler tubes. These problems can result in defluidization and in unwanted plant shutdowns, with reduced plant availability as a consequence. Research is currently being performed to explain and solve these
Fig. 1. Process scheme of the studied poultry manure combustion plant.

2: To determine whether combustion of poultry manure to pro- duce electricity is to be preferred over its direct land spreading, a consequential LCA approach is used. The functional unit of the assessment is treatment of 1 Mg of Dutch poultry manure. The relevant emissions from the poultry manure are included in the assessment from the moment the manure leaves the shed. Trans- port of the poultry manure to the combustion plant is included in the study, but transport of the manure for land application is not included, due to a lack of data. It should be noted though, that poultry farming occurs mainly in areas with lean soil, resulting in transport to more cultivated areas. This probably results in an un- derestimation of the impact of land spreading in this study. An overview of the processes included in this study is given in Fig. 2.

The goal of poultry farming is the production of meat or eggs, not the production of manure. A zero-burden approach can hence be used for the manure, meaning that the entire environmental impact of the poultry farming should be allocated to the products, meat or eggs. Indeed, a decreasing demand for meat or eggs does effectively reduce the number of poultry farms. On the other hand, it is clear that this is not the case for an increased demand for manure.

Apart from the operational emissions from land application or combustion, also upstream (e.g. production of Ca(OH)2 and NH3) and downstream (e.g. avoided production of electricity) processes are assessed. For the SCR, only the operational emissions (e.g. heat from natural gas) are included, without including the production of the catalyst. The production of capital goods of the combustion plant (i.e. construction materials) is not included, because their impacts per Mg of poultry manure are relatively low throughout the lifetime of the plant (Astrup et al., 2009), and are comparable to the impacts related to capital goods that would be needed to pro- duce the avoided electricity. The final use of the combustion products as inorganic soil conditioner is included in the environ- mental impact assessment.

The production of mineral N fertilizers may or may not be included in the study, depending on the type of application of the poultry manure ash. To date, there is a tendency to apply PK and N fertilizers separately (Lambert et al., 2006), e.g. the former in the fall, the latter in the spring. Furthermore, one might prefer the use of poultry manure ash as a PK fertilizer, allowing a better fit to the nutrient needs of the relevant soil and crop. However, we included the addition of a mineral N fertilizer, as a replacement of the poultry manure N, in this study as a worst case scenario for the impact of the combustion process. In that case, the amount of mineral N fertilizer (e.g. NH4NO3) is assumed to equal the amount of N from the poultry manure (Table 1), despite the lower loss of N mineral fertilizers upon land application (Bouwman and Boumans, 2002; Clayton et al., 1997), mainly due to the lower pH.
We argue that the loss of C from the poultry manure during combustion, has no negative influence on the value and the

environmental impact of the ash as a mineral fertilizer. It is doubtful whether C fertilization is always necessary, and if so, other C sources are available (e.g. compost) at a low price and with no environmental impact.

The functional unit of the assessment is treatment of 1 Mg of Dutch poultry manure. The relevant emissions from the poultry manure are included in the assessment from the moment the manure leaves the shed. Trans- port of the poultry manure to the combustion plant is included in the study, but transport of the manure for land application is not included, due to a lack of data. It should be noted though, that poultry farming occurs mainly in areas with lean soil, resulting in transport to more cultivated areas. This probably results in an un- derestimation of the impact of land spreading in this study. An overview of the processes included in this study is given in Fig. 2.

The goal of poultry farming is the production of meat or eggs, not the production of manure. A zero-burden approach can hence be used for the manure, meaning that the entire environmental impact of the poultry farming should be allocated to the products, meat or eggs. Indeed, a decreasing demand for meat or eggs does effectively reduce the number of poultry farms. On the other hand, it is clear that this is not the case for an increased demand for manure.

Apart from the operational emissions from land application or combustion, also upstream (e.g. production of Ca(OH)2 and NH3) and downstream (e.g. avoided production of electricity) processes are assessed. For the SCR, only the operational emissions (e.g. heat from natural gas) are included, without including the production of the catalyst. The production of capital goods of the combustion plant (i.e. construction materials) is not included, because their impacts per Mg of poultry manure are relatively low throughout the lifetime of the plant (Astrup et al., 2009), and are comparable to the impacts related to capital goods that would be needed to pro- duce the avoided electricity. The final use of the combustion products as inorganic soil conditioner is included in the environ- mental impact assessment.

The production of mineral N fertilizers may or may not be included in the study, depending on the type of application of the poultry manure ash. To date, there is a tendency to apply PK and N fertilizers separately (Lambert et al., 2006), e.g. the former in the fall, the latter in the spring. Furthermore, one might prefer the use of poultry manure ash as a PK fertilizer, allowing a better fit to the nutrient needs of the relevant soil and crop. However, we included the addition of a mineral N fertilizer, as a replacement of the poultry manure N, in this study as a worst case scenario for the impact of the combustion process. In that case, the amount of mineral N fertilizer (e.g. NH4NO3) is assumed to equal the amount of N from the poultry manure (Table 1), despite the lower loss of N mineral fertilizers upon land application (Bouwman and Boumans, 2002; Clayton et al., 1997), mainly due to the lower pH.
We argue that the loss of C from the poultry manure during combustion, has no negative influence on the value and the

environmental impact of the ash as a mineral fertilizer. It is doubtful whether C fertilization is always necessary, and if so, other C sources are available (e.g. compost) at a low price and with no environmental impact.

7: The emissions of CO, SO2, HCI, NO, NH3, PCDD/Fs and toxic metals from the poultry manure combustion plant were obtained from personal communications. CO2 emissions were not measured, but were calculated based on the average poultry manure compo- sition. N20 was not measured, but was estimated based on litera- ture data for combustion plants equipped with an SCR installation.

Operational data such as Ca(OH)2, NH3, sand and natural gas consumption are yearly averages until 2012. Data for the emissions from the production of Ca(OH)2, NH3 and heat from natural gas were obtained from the Ecoinvent v2.0 database using SimaPro.

Obtaining emission data for land spreading of manure is not straightforward and subject to large uncertainties, as the emissions are strongly dependent on the application method, grown crop, soil type, season, rainfall, etc. The emissions for land spreading of poultry manure are estimated based on literature data.
The environmental impact assessment is performed using the characterization factors from ReCiPe v1.08 Midpoint, according to the hierarchic perspective.
8: Despite its decreasing share in the energy production mix of the Netherlands, the combustion of coal still plays a significant role in this mix, though the largest energy source for the production of electricity is natural gas (CBS, 2014). Because the poultry manure combustion plant is constructed and operated very similarly to a coal combustion plant, and given the decreasing production of electricity of coal, it is likely that the electricity produced from poultry manure avoids emissions from coal combustion.

Since the emitted CO2 from poultry manure combustion is biogenic, the direct contribution of the energy production facility to global warming is very limited (Gentil et al., 2009). Moreover, the green electricity delivered to the electric grid avoids GHG emissions corresponding to 655 kg of CO2 equivalents per Mg of poultry manure, by replacing electricity produced from coal combustion. Other GHG emissions are CO and N20. The very low CO emissions (8.5 mg/Nm3 have no impact on global warming. N20 on the other hand, is a GHG with a high impact. The global warming potential (GWP) of N20 is 298 kg of CO2-equivalents per kg.

No N20 emission data are available for the studied power plant. For installations equipped with an SCR DeNOx, formation of N20 is a consequence of the equilibria involving mainly NH3, NO, NO2, N2, N20, O2 and H2O. N20 formation can result from the partial oxidation of NH3, or from the partial reduction of NOx by NH3 (Svoboda et al., 2006; Madia et al., 2002; Vermeulen et al., 2012), according to reactions (1-3), or by the decomposition of NH4NO3, which can be formed at temperatures below 200 °C (Madia et al., 2002).
Experimental data with respect to the reaction equilibria involved in SCR of NOx, covering both situations with high

concentrations (Martín et al., 2007) and with lower (300 ppm) concentrations (Goo et al., 2007) of NOx and NH3 in the raw flue gas, showed N20 emissions of 10-60 mg/Nm³ 3 for flue gas temperatures below 300 °C (Madia et al., 2002; Goo et al., 2007; Martín et al., 2007). This agrees also with the observations of N20 emissions from a municipal solid waste incinerator equipped with an SCR, which are 19-40 mg/Nm³ (Park et al., 2011).

For the poultry manure fired FBC installation, equipped with an SCR DeNOx operating at 195 °C, N20 emissions of 10-60 mg/Nm³ correspond to 44-260 g of N2O per Mg of fuel (poultry manure), based on a flue gas flow rate of 240,000 Nm³ and 8000 annual operating hours. The impact on global warming from N20 emis- sions for the studied plant can therefore be estimated at 13-78 kg CO2 equivalents per Mg of poultry manure combusted.

The direct GHG emissions from the land application of animal manure mainly consist of biogenic CO2 and of N20. For poultry manure, N20 losses range from 0.5 to 7.2% of total N applied (Sistani et al., 2011; Shepherd et al., 1991; Velthof et al., 2003; Pierzynski and Gehl, 2005; Prapaspongsa et al., 2010; Meade et al., 2011; Hyde et al., 2006; Thornton et al., 1998), but this is strongly dependent on the method of land application (Clayton et al., 1997; Prapaspongsa et al., 2010). Considering the N concentration in poultry manure to be 4.2 wt% DM, the emissions of N20 from land application are estimated 0.1-1.5 kg per Mg of poultry manure. Land application therefore causes an impact on global warming of 30-450 kg of CO2 equivalents per Mg of poultry manure.

Organic sequestration of carbon is not relevant in this case, as the fertilized land is commonly used for fast growing crops, meaning that the carbon will be emitted as CO2 after a short period of time (months). A comparison of the impact on climate change from combustion and land spreading of poultry manure is shown in Fig. 3. A minimum and maximum impact on climate change are provided for both treatment options of poultry manure, i.e. com- bustion and land spreading. In the worst case scenario for
Fig. 3. GHG accounting for combustion (left) and land spreading (right) of poultry manure. A minimum and maximum value is provided, to cover for different assump- tions and literature data.

combustion, the electricity produced avoids GHG emissions from natural gas combustion, instead of coal combustion. This corre- sponds to savings of 357 kg of CO2-equivalents per Mg of poultry manure, instead of 655 kg. The worst case scenario also includes the production of mineral N fertilizer to be added to the ash in the GHG accounting. Finally, the broad range of N20 emissions from litera- ture is reflected in the minimum and maximum impact for both treatment options.

It should be noted that the impact of combusting poultry manure on climate change is not limited to direct emissions of GHGs. Upstream emissions originate from the production of sand, lime and ammonia, start-up fuel, heat from natural gas and transport.

The low sulfur and chlorine concentration (Kelleher et al., 2002), in combination with the high calcium concentration of the poultry manure ash (binding sulfate and chloride) (Xie et al., 1999) result in a relatively low lime consumption in the flue gas cleaning instal- lation, approximately 1200 year The GHG emissions resulting from the production of this lime are 2.1 kg of CO2 equivalents per Mg of poultry manure combusted.

Transport of poultry manure to the combustion plant is on the average responsible for 14 kg of CO2 equivalents emitted per Mg, while export of the ash is responsible for another 10 kg of CO2 equivalents per Mg of poultry manure. The heating with natural gas for the SCR corresponds to the emission of 4.5 kg of CO2 equivalents per Mg of poultry manure. Finally, the production of 200 Mg of NH3 solution to be added to the flue gas in the SCR, is responsible for 0.2 kg of CO2 equivalents emitted to the atmosphere per Mg of poultry manure. The emissions from the SCR process (heat from natural gas and NH3 upstream production), are low in comparison with other GHG emissions and therefore nearly visible in Fig. 3. Also the emissions of the start-up fuel oil, 500 Mg year are very low with respect to the overall emissions.

It appears from Fig. 3 that the combustion of poultry manure results in a net reduction of GHG emissions, due to the large savings by replacing electricity from fossil fuels. On the other hand, direct land spreading of poultry manure increases the GHG emissions, because of the formation of N20.

A comparison of the impact on climate change from combustion and land spreading of poultry manure is shown in Fig. 3. A minimum and maximum impact on climate change are provided for both treatment options of poultry manure, i.e. com- bustion and land spreading. In the worst case scenario for
Fig. 3. GHG accounting for combustion (left) and land spreading (right) of poultry manure. A minimum and maximum value is provided, to cover for different assump- tions and literature data.

combustion, the electricity produced avoids GHG emissions from natural gas combustion, instead of coal combustion. This corre- sponds to savings of 357 kg of CO2-equivalents per Mg of poultry manure, instead of 655 kg. The worst case scenario also includes the production of mineral N fertilizer to be added to the ash in the GHG accounting. Finally, the broad range of N20 emissions from litera- ture is reflected in the minimum and maximum impact for both treatment options.

It should be noted that the impact of combusting poultry manure on climate change is not limited to direct emissions of GHGs. Upstream emissions originate from the production of sand, lime and ammonia, start-up fuel, heat from natural gas and transport.

The low sulfur and chlorine concentration (Kelleher et al., 2002), in combination with the high calcium concentration of the poultry manure ash (binding sulfate and chloride) (Xie et al., 1999) result in a relatively low lime consumption in the flue gas cleaning instal- lation, approximately 1200 year The GHG emissions resulting from the production of this lime are 2.1 kg of CO2 equivalents per Mg of poultry manure combusted.

Transport of poultry manure to the combustion plant is on the average responsible for 14 kg of CO2 equivalents emitted per Mg, while export of the ash is responsible for another 10 kg of CO2 equivalents per Mg of poultry manure. The heating with natural gas for the SCR corresponds to the emission of 4.5 kg of CO2 equivalents per Mg of poultry manure. Finally, the production of 200 Mg of NH3 solution to be added to the flue gas in the SCR, is responsible for 0.2 kg of CO2 equivalents emitted to the atmosphere per Mg of poultry manure. The emissions from the SCR process (heat from natural gas and NH3 upstream production), are low in comparison with other GHG emissions and therefore nearly visible in Fig. 3. Also the emissions of the start-up fuel oil, 500 Mg year are very low with respect to the overall emissions.

It appears from Fig. 3 that the combustion of poultry manure results in a net reduction of GHG emissions, due to the large savings by replacing electricity from fossil fuels. On the other hand, direct land spreading of poultry manure increases the GHG emissions, because of the formation of N20.
12: Despite its decreasing share in the energy production mix of the Netherlands, the combustion of coal still plays a significant role in this mix, though the largest energy source for the production of electricity is natural gas (CBS, 2014). Because the poultry manure combustion plant is constructed and operated very similarly to a coal combustion plant, and given the decreasing production of electricity of coal, it is likely that the electricity produced from poultry manure avoids emissions from coal combustion.

Since the emitted CO2 from poultry manure combustion is biogenic, the direct contribution of the energy production facility to global warming is very limited (Gentil et al., 2009). Moreover, the green electricity delivered to the electric grid avoids GHG emissions corresponding to 655 kg of CO2 equivalents per Mg of poultry manure, by replacing electricity produced from coal combustion. Other GHG emissions are CO and N20. The very low CO emissions (8.5 mg/Nm3 have no impact on global warming. N20 on the other hand, is a GHG with a high impact. The global warming potential (GWP) of N20 is 298 kg of CO2-equivalents per kg.

No N20 emission data are available for the studied power plant. For installations equipped with an SCR DeNOx, formation of N20 is a consequence of the equilibria involving mainly NH3, NO, NO2, N2, N20, O2 and H2O. N20 formation can result from the partial oxidation of NH3, or from the partial reduction of NOx by NH3 (Svoboda et al., 2006; Madia et al., 2002; Vermeulen et al., 2012), according to reactions (1-3), or by the decomposition of NH4NO3, which can be formed at temperatures below 200 °C (Madia et al., 2002).
Experimental data with respect to the reaction equilibria involved in SCR of NOx, covering both situations with high

concentrations (Martín et al., 2007) and with lower (300 ppm) concentrations (Goo et al., 2007) of NOx and NH3 in the raw flue gas, showed N20 emissions of 10-60 mg/Nm³ 3 for flue gas temperatures below 300 °C (Madia et al., 2002; Goo et al., 2007; Martín et al., 2007). This agrees also with the observations of N20 emissions from a municipal solid waste incinerator equipped with an SCR, which are 19-40 mg/Nm³ (Park et al., 2011).

For the poultry manure fired FBC installation, equipped with an SCR DeNOx operating at 195 °C, N20 emissions of 10-60 mg/Nm³ correspond to 44-260 g of N2O per Mg of fuel (poultry manure), based on a flue gas flow rate of 240,000 Nm³ and 8000 annual operating hours. The impact on global warming from N20 emis- sions for the studied plant can therefore be estimated at 13-78 kg CO2 equivalents per Mg of poultry manure combusted.

The direct GHG emissions from the land application of animal manure mainly consist of biogenic CO2 and of N20. For poultry manure, N20 losses range from 0.5 to 7.2% of total N applied (Sistani et al., 2011; Shepherd et al., 1991; Velthof et al., 2003; Pierzynski and Gehl, 2005; Prapaspongsa et al., 2010; Meade et al., 2011; Hyde et al., 2006; Thornton et al., 1998), but this is strongly dependent on the method of land application (Clayton et al., 1997; Prapaspongsa et al., 2010). Considering the N concentration in poultry manure to be 4.2 wt% DM, the emissions of N20 from land application are estimated 0.1-1.5 kg per Mg of poultry manure. Land application therefore causes an impact on global warming of 30-450 kg of CO2 equivalents per Mg of poultry manure.

Organic sequestration of carbon is not relevant in this case, as the fertilized land is commonly used for fast growing crops, meaning that the carbon will be emitted as CO2 after a short period of time (months). 16: The nature of the emissions from a biomass power plant is very different compared to the emissions from land spreading of manure. As previously mentioned, N20 emissions from land application of manure are significant. In addition, ammonia (NH3) is in most cases emitted to air both during the application (broad spreading) and during the first hours and days after spreading (Sommer and Hutchings, 2001; Meade et al., 2011). The losses due to NH3 volatilization range from 7% to 83% of the total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN) applied on land (Misselbrook et al., 2000; Sommer and Hutchings, 2001; Lockeyer et al., 1989; Prapaspongsa et al., 2010; Meade et al., 2011). Like most emissions from land spreading, NH3 volatilization is strongly dependent on factors like climate (e.g. temperature, rainfall intensity), soil type, season, crop type, application method and loading rate (Edwards and Daniel, 1992). Since NH3 has a high impact on terrestrial acidification and a moderate impact on marine eutrophication and particulate matter (PM10) formation, these emissions potentially cause envi- ronmental problems. It is stated that e.g. for the EU27, 85% of the NH3 emissions to atmosphere may be attributed to agriculture (Van Grinsven et al., 2013). Assuming a TAN concentration of 3 wt% in the studied manure, the NH3 emissions from land spreading are 3- 25 kg NH3 per Mg of poultry manure, whereas the emissions to air from the combustion plant are approximately 0.017 kg NH3 per Mg. Moreover, the production of electricity to replace electricity from
coal combustion reduces the net increase of NH3 emissions, as shown in Table 3.

Unlike most solid fuels, where nitrogen is usually organically bound (Vermeulen et al., 2012), a large fraction of the nitrogen in livestock manure is present as NH3-N (Edwards and Daniel, 1992). According to the mechanism described by Vermeulen et al. (2012), this NH3 reduces NO to N2 at temperatures of approximately 800 °C. This is the main reason for the low NH3 emissions from the installation. Because of the reactions with the NH3 present, the emissions of NOx are also low. Moreover, a selective catalytic reductor (SCR) before the stack reduces any excess NO in the raw flue gas, resulting in average NOx emissions below 50 mg/Nm³, which corresponds to 0.22 kg NOx per Mg of poultry manure. NOx has a lower impact on marine eutrophication, particulate matter formation and terrestrial acidification than NH3, but has also an impact on photochemical oxidant creation and is more toxic than NH3. However, the NO emissions from combusting poultry manure are lower than the NH3 emissions from land spreading, and lower than the average emission of NOx from coal and gas power plants in The Netherlands (Table resulting in a net environmental benefit for poultry manure power plants.

Moreover, several authors have measured or inventoried NOx emissions from fertilized agricultural land (Yamulki et al., 1997; Velthof et al., 2009; Akiyama and Tsuruta, 2003; Thornton et al., 1998). NO and NO2 are formed due to nitrification and denitrifi- cation processes in soil (Clayton et al., 1997). Reviewing the emis- sion data from 12 field observations, Veldkamp and Keller (1997) showed a linear relationship between the amount of fertilizer N applied and the NO emissions, resulting in a 95% confidence in- terval of 0.24-0.79% of fertilizer N being converted to NO. The N concentration of poultry manure is 4.2 wt% DM, so, taking into account the moisture content, the NO emissions of land spreading can be estimated at 0.12-0.39 kg per Mg of manure.

An overview of the input and output of the nitrogen compounds is provided in Table 2. Only the emissions included in this study are shown, other emissions are N2 to the atmosphere and NO3 to sur- face water.

Acid gas (HCI, SO2) emissions from the combustion of poultry manure are limited, due to the low sulfur and chlorine content of the fuel, the presence of a large excess of calcium components in the fluidized bed (Kelleher et al., 2002) and the acid scrubbing in the Turbosorp reactor by the reaction with lime. The average HCI and SO2 stack emissions are below 0.1 and 5 mg/Nm³, respectively, corresponding to 0.4 g and 20 g per Mg of poultry manure com- busted. The emissions of SO2 from poultry manure combustion are much lower than the avoided emissions from coal combustion (Table 3), resulting in a reduction of the environmental impact when poultry manure is combusted for electricity production.

The concentration of dioxins and furans (PCDD/Fs) in the flue gas is sufficiently low (lower than the detection limit of 0.001 ng TEQ/Nm³ so that no activated carbon is injected. The low PCDD/F formation compared to e.g. incineration of household waste can probably be attributed to the low chlorine and heavy metal (acting as a catalyst) concentrations in the poultry manure. In addition, also animal manure contains approximately 0.6 ng TEQ/kg DM, which is potentially transferred to the food chain when spread on land

|Unit|Direct (stack) emissions PM combustion|Avoided emissions from coal|Avoided emissions from gas |Unit|[Per Mg of wet poultry manure combusted]|[Per Mg of wet poultry manure combusted]|[Per Mg of wet poultry manure combusted] CO2, fossil|kg|0|626|351 CO|kg|0.05|0.15|0.08 NO|kg|0.22|1.10|0.34 NH3|g|17|27|0.03 SO2|g|20|830|4.9 HCI|g|4|8|0,01 Hg|mg|<4ª|12|0.37 Cd + Tl|mg|<9a|0.9|0.01 Sb, As, Cr, Co, Cu,|mg|<65|262|0.96 Pb, Mn, Ni, V PCDD/Fs (TEQ)|ng|<44|53|0.30
a Below the detection limit, so the provided value is an overestimation.

(Dumortier et al., 2012). The recycled ash mixture contains less than 5 ng TEQ per kg. However, because the mass of fertilizer used for land application is determined based on the concentration of the main nutrient, both concentrations should be corrected with respect to the concentration of P, resulting in approximately the same order of PCDD/F load on agricultural land. The stack emissions of toxic metals are regulated (BVA, 2004) and reported for Hg separately, for the sum of Cd and TI, and for the sum of Sb, As, Cr, Co, Cu, Pb, Mn, Ni and V. The average airborne emissions of the poultry manure FBC in Moerdijk are lower than 0.001, 0.002 and 0.015 mg/Nm³. This corresponds to 4 mg of Hg, 9 mg of Cd + Tl and 65 mg of other regulated metals per Mg of poultry manure. For the environmental assessment, the emissions of Hg, Cd, TI, Sb, As, Co and V, lower than the detection limit, were set equal to the detection limit of 0.001 mg/Nm³, resulting in an overestimation of the impact. The dust (PM10) emissions are 2 g per Mg of poultry manure.

It is known that poultry manure may cause diseases, due to the presence of pathogens (Edwards and Daniel, 1992). The emissions of these pathogens to air and water are a major disadvantage of land spreading as treatment option for poultry manure (O'Connor et al., 2005). The high turbulence of a fluidized bed combustor promotes efficient heat transfer and uniform mixing, resulting in efficient combustion (Van Caneghem et al., 2012), with destruction of the pathogens from the poultry manure. As the flue gas is sub- jected to temperatures over 850 °C, with a residence time of at least 2 S, no pathogens are expected to be emitted from the stack.

Whereas the emissions to water from a poultry manure com- bustion plant with the considered type of dry flue gas cleaning are negligible, land spreading of manure as fertilizer causes large emissions to surface and ground water. Among these emissions, the main pollutants are nitrate (NO), ammonium (NH4), organic N species (e.g. urea or uric acid), organic C. The environmental impact caused by these emissions is mainly in the impact category eutro- phication. Soluble phosphate is also slightly emitted to water, but this is also the case when combustion ash is used as soil conditioner or inorganic fertilizer. As the emissions to water depend to a large extent on several parameters, as described above for emissions to air, and emissions of N compounds to water also occur for mineral N fertilizers, these emissions are excluded from the impact assessment.

When assessing the environmental performance of the poultry manure combustion plant, upstream emissions of transport and lime, ammonia and sand production should be included, as well as process emissions from natural gas and start-up fuel oil combus- tion. These processes were discussed in Section 3.1, and the
resulting indirect environmental impact is included in the assess- ment in Section 3.4.
17: The nature of the emissions from a biomass power plant is very different compared to the emissions from land spreading of manure. As previously mentioned, N20 emissions from land application of manure are significant. In addition, ammonia (NH3) is in most cases emitted to air both during the application (broad spreading) and during the first hours and days after spreading (Sommer and Hutchings, 2001; Meade et al., 2011). The losses due to NH3 volatilization range from 7% to 83% of the total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN) applied on land (Misselbrook et al., 2000; Sommer and Hutchings, 2001; Lockeyer et al., 1989; Prapaspongsa et al., 2010; Meade et al., 2011). Like most emissions from land spreading, NH3 volatilization is strongly dependent on factors like climate (e.g. temperature, rainfall intensity), soil type, season, crop type, application method and loading rate (Edwards and Daniel, 1992). Since NH3 has a high impact on terrestrial acidification and a moderate impact on marine eutrophication and particulate matter (PM10) formation, these emissions potentially cause envi- ronmental problems. It is stated that e.g. for the EU27, 85% of the NH3 emissions to atmosphere may be attributed to agriculture (Van Grinsven et al., 2013). Assuming a TAN concentration of 3 wt% in the studied manure, the NH3 emissions from land spreading are 3- 25 kg NH3 per Mg of poultry manure, whereas the emissions to air from the combustion plant are approximately 0.017 kg NH3 per Mg. Moreover, the production of electricity to replace electricity from
coal combustion reduces the net increase of NH3 emissions, as shown in Table 3.

Unlike most solid fuels, where nitrogen is usually organically bound (Vermeulen et al., 2012), a large fraction of the nitrogen in livestock manure is present as NH3-N (Edwards and Daniel, 1992). According to the mechanism described by Vermeulen et al. (2012), this NH3 reduces NO to N2 at temperatures of approximately 800 °C. This is the main reason for the low NH3 emissions from the installation. Because of the reactions with the NH3 present, the emissions of NOx are also low. Moreover, a selective catalytic reductor (SCR) before the stack reduces any excess NO in the raw flue gas, resulting in average NOx emissions below 50 mg/Nm³, which corresponds to 0.22 kg NOx per Mg of poultry manure. NOx has a lower impact on marine eutrophication, particulate matter formation and terrestrial acidification than NH3, but has also an impact on photochemical oxidant creation and is more toxic than NH3. However, the NO emissions from combusting poultry manure are lower than the NH3 emissions from land spreading, and lower than the average emission of NOx from coal and gas power plants in The Netherlands (Table resulting in a net environmental benefit for poultry manure power plants.

Moreover, several authors have measured or inventoried NOx emissions from fertilized agricultural land (Yamulki et al., 1997; Velthof et al., 2009; Akiyama and Tsuruta, 2003; Thornton et al., 1998). NO and NO2 are formed due to nitrification and denitrifi- cation processes in soil (Clayton et al., 1997). Reviewing the emis- sion data from 12 field observations, Veldkamp and Keller (1997) showed a linear relationship between the amount of fertilizer N applied and the NO emissions, resulting in a 95% confidence in- terval of 0.24-0.79% of fertilizer N being converted to NO. The N concentration of poultry manure is 4.2 wt% DM, so, taking into account the moisture content, the NO emissions of land spreading can be estimated at 0.12-0.39 kg per Mg of manure.

An overview of the input and output of the nitrogen compounds is provided in Table 2. Only the emissions included in this study are shown, other emissions are N2 to the atmosphere and NO3 to sur- face water.

Acid gas (HCI, SO2) emissions from the combustion of poultry manure are limited, due to the low sulfur and chlorine content of the fuel, the presence of a large excess of calcium components in the fluidized bed (Kelleher et al., 2002) and the acid scrubbing in the Turbosorp reactor by the reaction with lime. The average HCI and SO2 stack emissions are below 0.1 and 5 mg/Nm³, respectively, corresponding to 0.4 g and 20 g per Mg of poultry manure com- busted. The emissions of SO2 from poultry manure combustion are much lower than the avoided emissions from coal combustion (Table 3), resulting in a reduction of the environmental impact when poultry manure is combusted for electricity production.

The concentration of dioxins and furans (PCDD/Fs) in the flue gas is sufficiently low (lower than the detection limit of 0.001 ng TEQ/Nm³ so that no activated carbon is injected. The low PCDD/F formation compared to e.g. incineration of household waste can probably be attributed to the low chlorine and heavy metal (acting as a catalyst) concentrations in the poultry manure. In addition, also animal manure contains approximately 0.6 ng TEQ/kg DM, which is potentially transferred to the food chain when spread on land

The losses due to NH3 volatilization range from 7% to 83% of the total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN) applied on land (Misselbrook et al., 2000; Sommer and Hutchings, 2001; Lockeyer et al., 1989; Prapaspongsa et al., 2010; Meade et al., 2011). Like most emissions from land spreading, NH3 volatilization is strongly dependent on factors like climate (e.g. temperature, rainfall intensity), soil type, season, crop type, application method and loading rate (Edwards and Daniel, 1992). Since NH3 has a high impact on terrestrial acidification and a moderate impact on marine eutrophication and particulate matter (PM10) formation, these emissions potentially cause envi- ronmental problems. It is stated that e.g. for the EU27, 85% of the NH3 emissions to atmosphere may be attributed to agriculture (Van Grinsven et al., 2013). Assuming a TAN concentration of 3 wt% in the studied manure, the NH3 emissions from land spreading are 3- 25 kg NH3 per Mg of poultry manure, whereas the emissions to air from the combustion plant are approximately 0.017 kg NH3 per Mg. Moreover, the production of electricity to replace electricity from
coal combustion reduces the net increase of NH3 emissions, as shown in Table 3.

Unlike most solid fuels, where nitrogen is usually organically bound (Vermeulen et al., 2012), a large fraction of the nitrogen in livestock manure is present as NH3-N (Edwards and Daniel, 1992). According to the mechanism described by Vermeulen et al. (2012), this NH3 reduces NO to N2 at temperatures of approximately 800 °C. This is the main reason for the low NH3 emissions from the installation. Because of the reactions with the NH3 present, the emissions of NOx are also low. Moreover, a selective catalytic reductor (SCR) before the stack reduces any excess NO in the raw flue gas, resulting in average NOx emissions below 50 mg/Nm³, which corresponds to 0.22 kg NOx per Mg of poultry manure. NOx has a lower impact on marine eutrophication, particulate matter formation and terrestrial acidification than NH3, but has also an impact on photochemical oxidant creation and is more toxic than NH3. However, the NO emissions from combusting poultry manure are lower than the NH3 emissions from land spreading, and lower than the average emission of NOx from coal and gas power plants in The Netherlands (Table resulting in a net environmental benefit for poultry manure power plants.

Moreover, several authors have measured or inventoried NOx emissions from fertilized agricultural land (Yamulki et al., 1997; Velthof et al., 2009; Akiyama and Tsuruta, 2003; Thornton et al., 1998). NO and NO2 are formed due to nitrification and denitrifi- cation processes in soil (Clayton et al., 1997). Reviewing the emis- sion data from 12 field observations, Veldkamp and Keller (1997) showed a linear relationship between the amount of fertilizer N applied and the NO emissions, resulting in a 95% confidence in- terval of 0.24-0.79% of fertilizer N being converted to NO. The N concentration of poultry manure is 4.2 wt% DM, so, taking into account the moisture content, the NO emissions of land spreading can be estimated at 0.12-0.39 kg per Mg of manure.

An overview of the input and output of the nitrogen compounds is provided in Table 2. Only the emissions included in this study are shown, other emissions are N2 to the atmosphere and NO3 to sur- face water.

Acid gas (HCI, SO2) emissions from the combustion of poultry manure are limited, due to the low sulfur and chlorine content of the fuel, the presence of a large excess of calcium components in the fluidized bed (Kelleher et al., 2002) and the acid scrubbing in the Turbosorp reactor by the reaction with lime. The average HCI and SO2 stack emissions are below 0.1 and 5 mg/Nm³, respectively, corresponding to 0.4 g and 20 g per Mg of poultry manure com- busted. The emissions of SO2 from poultry manure combustion are much lower than the avoided emissions from coal combustion (Table 3), resulting in a reduction of the environmental impact when poultry manure is combusted for electricity production.

The concentration of dioxins and furans (PCDD/Fs) in the flue gas is sufficiently low (lower than the detection limit of 0.001 ng TEQ/Nm³ so that no activated carbon is injected. The low PCDD/F formation compared to e.g. incineration of household waste can probably be attributed to the low chlorine and heavy metal (acting as a catalyst) concentrations in the poultry manure. In addition, also animal manure contains approximately 0.6 ng TEQ/kg DM, which is potentially transferred to the food chain when spread on land
Overview of the atmospheric emissions for two alternative treatment scenarios, based on the treatment of 1 Mg of poultry manure.

1 Mg of poultry manure|1 Mg of poultry manure|Atmospheric emissions from land spreading|Atmospheric emissions from combustion Total N|42 kg|N20 0.1-1.5 kg|N20 0.044-0.26 kg TAN|30 kg|NH3 3-25 kg|NH3 0.017 kg NO3-N|<1.5 kg|NO 0.12-0.39 kg|NO 0.22 kg
Table 3 Stack emission data from the combustion of poultry manure (PM) and avoided emissions from replacement of electricity from fossil fuels in The Netherlands.

|Unit|Direct (stack) emissions PM combustion|Avoided emissions from coal|Avoided emissions from gas |Unit|[Per Mg of wet poultry manure combusted]|[Per Mg of wet poultry manure combusted]|[Per Mg of wet poultry manure combusted] CO2, fossil|kg|0|626|351 CO|kg|0.05|0.15|0.08 NO|kg|0.22|1.10|0.34 NH3|g|17|27|0.03 SO2|g|20|830|4.9 HCI|g|4|8|0,01 Hg|mg|<4ª|12|0.37 Cd + Tl|mg|<9a|0.9|0.01 Sb, As, Cr, Co, Cu,|mg|<65|262|0.96 Pb, Mn, Ni, V PCDD/Fs (TEQ)|ng|<44|53|0.30
a Below the detection limit, so the provided value is an overestimation.

(Dumortier et al., 2012). The recycled ash mixture contains less than 5 ng TEQ per kg. However, because the mass of fertilizer used for land application is determined based on the concentration of the main nutrient, both concentrations should be corrected with respect to the concentration of P, resulting in approximately the same order of PCDD/F load on agricultural land. The stack emissions of toxic metals are regulated (BVA, 2004) and reported for Hg separately, for the sum of Cd and TI, and for the sum of Sb, As, Cr, Co, Cu, Pb, Mn, Ni and V. The average airborne emissions of the poultry manure FBC in Moerdijk are lower than 0.001, 0.002 and 0.015 mg/Nm³. This corresponds to 4 mg of Hg, 9 mg of Cd + Tl and 65 mg of other regulated metals per Mg of poultry manure. For the environmental assessment, the emissions of Hg, Cd, TI, Sb, As, Co and V, lower than the detection limit, were set equal to the detection limit of 0.001 mg/Nm³, resulting in an overestimation of the impact. The dust (PM10) emissions are 2 g per Mg of poultry manure.

It is known that poultry manure may cause diseases, due to the presence of pathogens (Edwards and Daniel, 1992). The emissions of these pathogens to air and water are a major disadvantage of land spreading as treatment option for poultry manure (O'Connor et al., 2005). The high turbulence of a fluidized bed combustor promotes efficient heat transfer and uniform mixing, resulting in efficient combustion (Van Caneghem et al., 2012), with destruction of the pathogens from the poultry manure. As the flue gas is sub- jected to temperatures over 850 °C, with a residence time of at least 2 S, no pathogens are expected to be emitted from the stack.

Whereas the emissions to water from a poultry manure com- bustion plant with the considered type of dry flue gas cleaning are negligible, land spreading of manure as fertilizer causes large emissions to surface and ground water. Among these emissions, the main pollutants are nitrate (NO), ammonium (NH4), organic N species (e.g. urea or uric acid), organic C. The environmental impact caused by these emissions is mainly in the impact category eutro- phication. Soluble phosphate is also slightly emitted to water, but this is also the case when combustion ash is used as soil conditioner or inorganic fertilizer. As the emissions to water depend to a large extent on several parameters, as described above for emissions to air, and emissions of N compounds to water also occur for mineral N fertilizers, these emissions are excluded from the impact assessment.

When assessing the environmental performance of the poultry manure combustion plant, upstream emissions of transport and lime, ammonia and sand production should be included, as well as process emissions from natural gas and start-up fuel oil combus- tion. These processes were discussed in Section 3.1, and the
resulting indirect environmental impact is included in the assess- ment in Section 3.4.

|Unit|Direct (stack) emissions PM combustion|Avoided emissions from coal|Avoided emissions from gas |Unit|[Per Mg of wet poultry manure combusted]|[Per Mg of wet poultry manure combusted]|[Per Mg of wet poultry manure combusted] CO2, fossil|kg|0|626|351 CO|kg|0.05|0.15|0.08 NO|kg|0.22|1.10|0.34 NH3|g|17|27|0.03 SO2|g|20|830|4.9 HCI|g|4|8|0,01 Hg|mg|<4ª|12|0.37 Cd + Tl|mg|<9a|0.9|0.01 Sb, As, Cr, Co, Cu,|mg|<65|262|0.96 Pb, Mn, Ni, V PCDD/Fs (TEQ)|ng|<44|53|0.30
a Below the detection limit, so the provided value is an overestimation.

(Dumortier et al., 2012). The recycled ash mixture contains less than 5 ng TEQ per kg. However, because the mass of fertilizer used for land application is determined based on the concentration of the main nutrient, both concentrations should be corrected with respect to the concentration of P, resulting in approximately the same order of PCDD/F load on agricultural land. The stack emissions of toxic metals are regulated (BVA, 2004) and reported for Hg separately, for the sum of Cd and TI, and for the sum of Sb, As, Cr, Co, Cu, Pb, Mn, Ni and V. The average airborne emissions of the poultry manure FBC in Moerdijk are lower than 0.001, 0.002 and 0.015 mg/Nm³. This corresponds to 4 mg of Hg, 9 mg of Cd + Tl and 65 mg of other regulated metals per Mg of poultry manure. For the environmental assessment, the emissions of Hg, Cd, TI, Sb, As, Co and V, lower than the detection limit, were set equal to the detection limit of 0.001 mg/Nm³, resulting in an overestimation of the impact. The dust (PM10) emissions are 2 g per Mg of poultry manure.

It is known that poultry manure may cause diseases, due to the presence of pathogens (Edwards and Daniel, 1992). The emissions of these pathogens to air and water are a major disadvantage of land spreading as treatment option for poultry manure (O'Connor et al., 2005). The high turbulence of a fluidized bed combustor promotes efficient heat transfer and uniform mixing, resulting in efficient combustion (Van Caneghem et al., 2012), with destruction of the pathogens from the poultry manure. As the flue gas is sub- jected to temperatures over 850 °C, with a residence time of at least 2 S, no pathogens are expected to be emitted from the stack.

Whereas the emissions to water from a poultry manure com- bustion plant with the considered type of dry flue gas cleaning are negligible, land spreading of manure as fertilizer causes large emissions to surface and ground water. Among these emissions, the main pollutants are nitrate (NO), ammonium (NH4), organic N species (e.g. urea or uric acid), organic C. The environmental impact caused by these emissions is mainly in the impact category eutro- phication. Soluble phosphate is also slightly emitted to water, but this is also the case when combustion ash is used as soil conditioner or inorganic fertilizer. As the emissions to water depend to a large extent on several parameters, as described above for emissions to air, and emissions of N compounds to water also occur for mineral N fertilizers, these emissions are excluded from the impact assessment.

When assessing the environmental performance of the poultry manure combustion plant, upstream emissions of transport and lime, ammonia and sand production should be included, as well as process emissions from natural gas and start-up fuel oil combus- tion. These processes were discussed in Section 3.1, and the
resulting indirect environmental impact is included in the assess- ment in Section 3.4.
24: The nature of the emissions from a biomass power plant is very different compared to the emissions from land spreading of manure. As previously mentioned, N20 emissions from land application of manure are significant. In addition, ammonia (NH3) is in most cases emitted to air both during the application (broad spreading) and during the first hours and days after spreading (Sommer and Hutchings, 2001; Meade et al., 2011). The losses due to NH3 volatilization range from 7% to 83% of the total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN) applied on land (Misselbrook et al., 2000; Sommer and Hutchings, 2001; Lockeyer et al., 1989; Prapaspongsa et al., 2010; Meade et al., 2011). Like most emissions from land spreading, NH3 volatilization is strongly dependent on factors like climate (e.g. temperature, rainfall intensity), soil type, season, crop type, application method and loading rate (Edwards and Daniel, 1992). Since NH3 has a high impact on terrestrial acidification and a moderate impact on marine eutrophication and particulate matter (PM10) formation, these emissions potentially cause envi- ronmental problems. It is stated that e.g. for the EU27, 85% of the NH3 emissions to atmosphere may be attributed to agriculture (Van Grinsven et al., 2013). Assuming a TAN concentration of 3 wt% in the studied manure, the NH3 emissions from land spreading are 3- 25 kg NH3 per Mg of poultry manure, whereas the emissions to air from the combustion plant are approximately 0.017 kg NH3 per Mg. Moreover, the production of electricity to replace electricity from
coal combustion reduces the net increase of NH3 emissions, as shown in Table 3.

Unlike most solid fuels, where nitrogen is usually organically bound (Vermeulen et al., 2012), a large fraction of the nitrogen in livestock manure is present as NH3-N (Edwards and Daniel, 1992). According to the mechanism described by Vermeulen et al. (2012), this NH3 reduces NO to N2 at temperatures of approximately 800 °C. This is the main reason for the low NH3 emissions from the installation. Because of the reactions with the NH3 present, the emissions of NOx are also low. Moreover, a selective catalytic reductor (SCR) before the stack reduces any excess NO in the raw flue gas, resulting in average NOx emissions below 50 mg/Nm³, which corresponds to 0.22 kg NOx per Mg of poultry manure. NOx has a lower impact on marine eutrophication, particulate matter formation and terrestrial acidification than NH3, but has also an impact on photochemical oxidant creation and is more toxic than NH3. However, the NO emissions from combusting poultry manure are lower than the NH3 emissions from land spreading, and lower than the average emission of NOx from coal and gas power plants in The Netherlands (Table resulting in a net environmental benefit for poultry manure power plants.

Moreover, several authors have measured or inventoried NOx emissions from fertilized agricultural land (Yamulki et al., 1997; Velthof et al., 2009; Akiyama and Tsuruta, 2003; Thornton et al., 1998). NO and NO2 are formed due to nitrification and denitrifi- cation processes in soil (Clayton et al., 1997). Reviewing the emis- sion data from 12 field observations, Veldkamp and Keller (1997) showed a linear relationship between the amount of fertilizer N applied and the NO emissions, resulting in a 95% confidence in- terval of 0.24-0.79% of fertilizer N being converted to NO. The N concentration of poultry manure is 4.2 wt% DM, so, taking into account the moisture content, the NO emissions of land spreading can be estimated at 0.12-0.39 kg per Mg of manure.

An overview of the input and output of the nitrogen compounds is provided in Table 2. Only the emissions included in this study are shown, other emissions are N2 to the atmosphere and NO3 to sur- face water.

Acid gas (HCI, SO2) emissions from the combustion of poultry manure are limited, due to the low sulfur and chlorine content of the fuel, the presence of a large excess of calcium components in the fluidized bed (Kelleher et al., 2002) and the acid scrubbing in the Turbosorp reactor by the reaction with lime. The average HCI and SO2 stack emissions are below 0.1 and 5 mg/Nm³, respectively, corresponding to 0.4 g and 20 g per Mg of poultry manure com- busted. The emissions of SO2 from poultry manure combustion are much lower than the avoided emissions from coal combustion (Table 3), resulting in a reduction of the environmental impact when poultry manure is combusted for electricity production.

The concentration of dioxins and furans (PCDD/Fs) in the flue gas is sufficiently low (lower than the detection limit of 0.001 ng TEQ/Nm³ so that no activated carbon is injected. The low PCDD/F formation compared to e.g. incineration of household waste can probably be attributed to the low chlorine and heavy metal (acting as a catalyst) concentrations in the poultry manure. In addition, also animal manure contains approximately 0.6 ng TEQ/kg DM, which is potentially transferred to the food chain when spread on land

The losses due to NH3 volatilization range from 7% to 83% of the total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN) applied on land (Misselbrook et al., 2000; Sommer and Hutchings, 2001; Lockeyer et al., 1989; Prapaspongsa et al., 2010; Meade et al., 2011). Like most emissions from land spreading, NH3 volatilization is strongly dependent on factors like climate (e.g. temperature, rainfall intensity), soil type, season, crop type, application method and loading rate (Edwards and Daniel, 1992). Since NH3 has a high impact on terrestrial acidification and a moderate impact on marine eutrophication and particulate matter (PM10) formation, these emissions potentially cause envi- ronmental problems. It is stated that e.g. for the EU27, 85% of the NH3 emissions to atmosphere may be attributed to agriculture (Van Grinsven et al., 2013). Assuming a TAN concentration of 3 wt% in the studied manure, the NH3 emissions from land spreading are 3- 25 kg NH3 per Mg of poultry manure, whereas the emissions to air from the combustion plant are approximately 0.017 kg NH3 per Mg. Moreover, the production of electricity to replace electricity from
coal combustion reduces the net increase of NH3 emissions, as shown in Table 3.

Unlike most solid fuels, where nitrogen is usually organically bound (Vermeulen et al., 2012), a large fraction of the nitrogen in livestock manure is present as NH3-N (Edwards and Daniel, 1992). According to the mechanism described by Vermeulen et al. (2012), this NH3 reduces NO to N2 at temperatures of approximately 800 °C. This is the main reason for the low NH3 emissions from the installation. Because of the reactions with the NH3 present, the emissions of NOx are also low. Moreover, a selective catalytic reductor (SCR) before the stack reduces any excess NO in the raw flue gas, resulting in average NOx emissions below 50 mg/Nm³, which corresponds to 0.22 kg NOx per Mg of poultry manure. NOx has a lower impact on marine eutrophication, particulate matter formation and terrestrial acidification than NH3, but has also an impact on photochemical oxidant creation and is more toxic than NH3. However, the NO emissions from combusting poultry manure are lower than the NH3 emissions from land spreading, and lower than the average emission of NOx from coal and gas power plants in The Netherlands (Table resulting in a net environmental benefit for poultry manure power plants.

Moreover, several authors have measured or inventoried NOx emissions from fertilized agricultural land (Yamulki et al., 1997; Velthof et al., 2009; Akiyama and Tsuruta, 2003; Thornton et al., 1998). NO and NO2 are formed due to nitrification and denitrifi- cation processes in soil (Clayton et al., 1997). Reviewing the emis- sion data from 12 field observations, Veldkamp and Keller (1997) showed a linear relationship between the amount of fertilizer N applied and the NO emissions, resulting in a 95% confidence in- terval of 0.24-0.79% of fertilizer N being converted to NO. The N concentration of poultry manure is 4.2 wt% DM, so, taking into account the moisture content, the NO emissions of land spreading can be estimated at 0.12-0.39 kg per Mg of manure.

An overview of the input and output of the nitrogen compounds is provided in Table 2. Only the emissions included in this study are shown, other emissions are N2 to the atmosphere and NO3 to sur- face water.

Acid gas (HCI, SO2) emissions from the combustion of poultry manure are limited, due to the low sulfur and chlorine content of the fuel, the presence of a large excess of calcium components in the fluidized bed (Kelleher et al., 2002) and the acid scrubbing in the Turbosorp reactor by the reaction with lime. The average HCI and SO2 stack emissions are below 0.1 and 5 mg/Nm³, respectively, corresponding to 0.4 g and 20 g per Mg of poultry manure com- busted. The emissions of SO2 from poultry manure combustion are much lower than the avoided emissions from coal combustion (Table 3), resulting in a reduction of the environmental impact when poultry manure is combusted for electricity production.

The concentration of dioxins and furans (PCDD/Fs) in the flue gas is sufficiently low (lower than the detection limit of 0.001 ng TEQ/Nm³ so that no activated carbon is injected. The low PCDD/F formation compared to e.g. incineration of household waste can probably be attributed to the low chlorine and heavy metal (acting as a catalyst) concentrations in the poultry manure. In addition, also animal manure contains approximately 0.6 ng TEQ/kg DM, which is potentially transferred to the food chain when spread on land

(Dumortier et al., 2012). The recycled ash mixture contains less than 5 ng TEQ per kg. However, because the mass of fertilizer used for land application is determined based on the concentration of the main nutrient, both concentrations should be corrected with respect to the concentration of P, resulting in approximately the same order of PCDD/F load on agricultural land. The stack emissions of toxic metals are regulated (BVA, 2004) and reported for Hg separately, for the sum of Cd and TI, and for the sum of Sb, As, Cr, Co, Cu, Pb, Mn, Ni and V. The average airborne emissions of the poultry manure FBC in Moerdijk are lower than 0.001, 0.002 and 0.015 mg/Nm³. This corresponds to 4 mg of Hg, 9 mg of Cd + Tl and 65 mg of other regulated metals per Mg of poultry manure. For the environmental assessment, the emissions of Hg, Cd, TI, Sb, As, Co and V, lower than the detection limit, were set equal to the detection limit of 0.001 mg/Nm³, resulting in an overestimation of the impact. The dust (PM10) emissions are 2 g per Mg of poultry manure.

It is known that poultry manure may cause diseases, due to the presence of pathogens (Edwards and Daniel, 1992). The emissions of these pathogens to air and water are a major disadvantage of land spreading as treatment option for poultry manure (O'Connor et al., 2005). The high turbulence of a fluidized bed combustor promotes efficient heat transfer and uniform mixing, resulting in efficient combustion (Van Caneghem et al., 2012), with destruction of the pathogens from the poultry manure. As the flue gas is sub- jected to temperatures over 850 °C, with a residence time of at least 2 S, no pathogens are expected to be emitted from the stack.

Whereas the emissions to water from a poultry manure com- bustion plant with the considered type of dry flue gas cleaning are negligible, land spreading of manure as fertilizer causes large emissions to surface and ground water. Among these emissions, the main pollutants are nitrate (NO), ammonium (NH4), organic N species (e.g. urea or uric acid), organic C. The environmental impact caused by these emissions is mainly in the impact category eutro- phication. Soluble phosphate is also slightly emitted to water, but this is also the case when combustion ash is used as soil conditioner or inorganic fertilizer. As the emissions to water depend to a large extent on several parameters, as described above for emissions to air, and emissions of N compounds to water also occur for mineral N fertilizers, these emissions are excluded from the impact assessment.

When assessing the environmental performance of the poultry manure combustion plant, upstream emissions of transport and lime, ammonia and sand production should be included, as well as process emissions from natural gas and start-up fuel oil combus- tion. These processes were discussed in Section 3.1, and the
resulting indirect environmental impact is included in the assess- ment in Section 3.4.
28: In the EU, regulation EC 1774/2002 and directive 91/676/EEC allow the application of livestock manure on agricultural land un- der certain conditions. The requirements are compliance with maximum allowable N and P application per area of agricultural land and absence of pathogens that constitute a hazard to public health. For manure, no legal restrictions are made concerning the concentration of heavy metals and organic pollutants.

On the other hand, inorganic fertilizers, regulated by EC 2003/ 2003, are classified as 'EC Fertilizer' or 'Other inorganic fertilizer', according to e.g. the Dutch legislation (Uitvoeringsbesluit Mest- stoffenwet, Nov. 9th, 2005). For the second category, environmental standards are set for the maximum allowable heavy metal con- centrations with respect to the value creating component (e.g. N, P2O5, K2O, neutralizing capacity, organic content).

To assess the suitability of the residues from the combustion of poultry manure for use as a fertilizer, attention must be given to: (i) the nutrient value of the inorganic fertilizer and its practical use, and (ii) the environmental impact of ash recycling.
29: <header>3.3.1. Nutrient value of poultry manure ash </header>

The bottom ash, the boiler ash and the flue gas cleaning residue from the combustion plant are recycled together as a soil condi- tioner. The nutrient value of poultry manure ash differs from the initial fuel (untreated poultry manure), as N compounds in the poultry manure are volatilized and transformed into N2 after the SCR DeNOx process, with only small amounts of NH3, N20 and NO remaining. The phosphate and potassium of the fuel are, however, almost entirely retained in the ash. The solubility of the phosphate in water and even in 2% citric acid for manure ash is relatively low (Thygesen and Johnsen, 2012). Specifically for the studied poultry manure ash this is determined to be on average 0.1% and 5%, respectively, while the total phosphate (P2O5) concentration is on the average 12%. Codling (2006) recommends amending poultry manure ash especially to low-pH and low-P soils. When poultry manure ash is applied to soil, the soil P concentration is increased, without drastically increasing the risk of runoff, as is the case for untreated manure. Codling et al. (2002) suggest that poultry manure ash is not significantly different from potassium phosphate as a fertilizer. The biomass growth and P uptake from poultry manure ash is as high as, or even higher than, the ones from potassium or calcium phosphate amended soil (Codling et al., 2002; Codling, 2013).

Practically, poultry manure ash has several advantages over untreated manure. The fact that (2% citric acid) soluble phosphate and potassium remain in the ash, while nitrogen is removed, makes the poultry manure ash de facto a PK fertilizer. This makes adjustment to the specific fertilization needs more straightforward. Harmful gaseous emissions (e.g. NH3) are avoided to a large extent due to the absence of nitrogen compounds in the ash. Moreover, in the case of N fertilization using mineral fertilizers (calcium ammonium nitrate, ammonium nitrate, ammonium sulfate, cal- cium nitrate), NH3 and N20 emissions are significantly lower than using manure (Hamelin et al., 2012; Clayton et al., 1997).
In addition, the ash is odorless, dry and free of pathogens, and thus more suitable for transport and storage than the fresh manure.

As there is a large excess of phosphate on agricultural land in the Netherlands (Csathó and Radimszky, 2009; Reijneveld et al., 2010), the ash of the studied poultry manure combustion plant is currently not applied on Dutch agricultural land. However, the beneficial properties of the ash over the fresh poultry manure, allow export to regions with a phosphate deficit. For the studied

combustion plant, the ash is exported to the United Kingdom, France and Germany. Potential markets with a negative P balance for the period 1991-2005 are e.g. Slovakia, Hungary, Bulgaria and Ukraine (Csathó and Radimszky, 2009). Combustion of poultry manure may therefore be considered a means to regulate the phosphate balance across regions.

32: Ash from poultry manure combustion can be recycled as a soil conditioner, with phosphate and potassium as macronutrients. National legislation concerning the land application of poultry manure ash differs throughout the EU, and will therefore not be discussed. Mostly, standards are set for the heavy metal concen- tration of the ash and in some countries the studied ash complies with these standards.

Nevertheless, land application of poultry manure ash does not increase the environmental impact compared to direct poultry manure spreading. The amount of heavy metals in the ash, with respect to the amount of phosphate, is similar to that of fresh poultry manure. The applied fertilization rate is determined based on the phosphorus concentration, so the amount of heavy metals that is spread per hectare of land remains the same.
33: From Section 3.2, it is clear that also land spreading of manure gives rise to large emissions of e.g. NH3, N20 and NOx. These emissions have an impact on climate change, terrestrial acidifica- tion, particulate matter formation, marine eutrophication and photochemical oxidant formation. In Fig. 4, the environmental impact is shown in several impact categories (climate change (CC), ozone depletion (Ozone Depl.), human toxicity (HT), photochemical oxidant formation (POF), particulate matter formation (PMF), terrestrial acidification (T. Acid.), marine eutrophication (M. Eutr.), terrestrial ecotoxicity (TET), freshwater exotoxicity (FET), marine water ecotoxicity (MET) and fossil depletion (Fossil Depl.)), normalized using data of the impact per inhabitant from the EU25+3 from Wagener Sleeswijk et al. (2008). The values in Fig. 4 corre- spond to the average of the provided data ranges from literature, as in Section 3.2. The error bars do not represent the standard devi- ation, but the minimum and maximum values of the literature ranges. The environmental impact of combustion of poultry manure is calculated for two scenarios, i.e. replacement of elec- tricity from gas and electricity from coal. As a worst case scenario, discussed in Section 2.2.1 and analogously to GHG accounting, the production of mineral N fertilizer is included in the scenario in which the produced electricity from poultry manure combustion substitutes electricity from natural gas combustion.

Fig. 4 shows that the emissions of land applied poultry manure constitute a large environmental impact. When electricity from coal is replaced, almost all categories show a negative value, meaning that electricity from poultry manure is an environmentally benefi- cial energy source, reducing the environmental impact. Even when electricity from gas is assumed to be replaced by electricity from poultry manure combustion, the impact is low, and the comparison with the alternative, land spreading, shows that poultry manure combustion avoids large impacts on acidification, particulate matter formation, climate change and marine eutrophication.

35: The environmental impact of two treatment options for poultry manure was compared; combustion with subsequent recycling of the ash and direct land spreading of the manure. In contrast to previous research of e.g. Reijnders and Huijbregts (2005), a zero-

Fig. 4. Environmental impact normalized for EU25+3 in 11 impact categories, for direct land spreading of poultry manure and for combustion of poultry manure according to two scenarios: (i) with replacement of electricity from gas, including production of NH4NO3, and (ii) with replacement of electricity from coal. The lower graph is an enlargement of the low normalized impacts. The error bars represent minimum and maximum values based on literature data.

burden approach was used in this paper, meaning that the impact of producing the manure (animal husbandry) is not taken into ac- count. Indeed, poultry is bred for its meat or eggs, not for its manure. Moreover, the impact of animal husbandry is identical for all of the compared treatment scenarios.

Combustion of poultry manure has a low environmental impact compared to land spreading, which is considered the most com- mon treatment option for manure. The recovery of energy to pro- duce electricity strongly reduces the impact of combustion in the impact categories climate change and fossil depletion, even when the produced electricity substitutes electricity from natural gas combustion. In the same worst case scenario (i.e. substitution of electricity from natural gas) for combustion, the production of mineral N fertilizer, to replace the N from the combusted poultry manure, did not increase the environmental impact significantly compared to land spreading. Indeed, the large atmospheric emis- sions of NH3, NOx and N20 that occur when manure is spread on agricultural land, cause a high environmental impact in the cate- gories terrestrial acidification, particulate matter formation, marine eutrophication and climate change.

The physical and chemical properties of poultry manure allow direct combustion in a fluidized bed combustor. However, for other manure types, e.g. pig and dairy cattle manure, pre-treatment is necessary, or other treatment techniques can be used. To evaluate their environmental performance, the key parameters are NH3, NOx and N20 emissions, as well as substitution of fossil energy (De Vries et al., 2012), similarly to the evaluation for poultry manure in this paper.

36: The environmental impact of two treatment options for poultry manure was compared; combustion with subsequent recycling of the ash and direct land spreading of the manure. In contrast to previous research of e.g. Reijnders and Huijbregts (2005), a zero-

Fig. 4. Environmental impact normalized for EU25+3 in 11 impact categories, for direct land spreading of poultry manure and for combustion of poultry manure according to two scenarios: (i) with replacement of electricity from gas, including production of NH4NO3, and (ii) with replacement of electricity from coal. The lower graph is an enlargement of the low normalized impacts. The error bars represent minimum and maximum values based on literature data.

burden approach was used in this paper, meaning that the impact of producing the manure (animal husbandry) is not taken into ac- count. Indeed, poultry is bred for its meat or eggs, not for its manure. Moreover, the impact of animal husbandry is identical for all of the compared treatment scenarios.

Combustion of poultry manure has a low environmental impact compared to land spreading, which is considered the most com- mon treatment option for manure. The recovery of energy to pro- duce electricity strongly reduces the impact of combustion in the impact categories climate change and fossil depletion, even when the produced electricity substitutes electricity from natural gas combustion. In the same worst case scenario (i.e. substitution of electricity from natural gas) for combustion, the production of mineral N fertilizer, to replace the N from the combusted poultry manure, did not increase the environmental impact significantly compared to land spreading. Indeed, the large atmospheric emis- sions of NH3, NOx and N20 that occur when manure is spread on agricultural land, cause a high environmental impact in the cate- gories terrestrial acidification, particulate matter formation, marine eutrophication and climate change.

The physical and chemical properties of poultry manure allow direct combustion in a fluidized bed combustor. However, for other manure types, e.g. pig and dairy cattle manure, pre-treatment is necessary, or other treatment techniques can be used. To evaluate their environmental performance, the key parameters are NH3, NOx and N20 emissions, as well as substitution of fossil energy (De Vries et al., 2012), similarly to the evaluation for poultry manure in this paper.

38: The environmental impact of two treatment options for poultry manure was compared; combustion with subsequent recycling of the ash and direct land spreading of the manure. In contrast to previous research of e.g. Reijnders and Huijbregts (2005), a zero-

Fig. 4. Environmental impact normalized for EU25+3 in 11 impact categories, for direct land spreading of poultry manure and for combustion of poultry manure according to two scenarios: (i) with replacement of electricity from gas, including production of NH4NO3, and (ii) with replacement of electricity from coal. The lower graph is an enlargement of the low normalized impacts. The error bars represent minimum and maximum values based on literature data.

burden approach was used in this paper, meaning that the impact of producing the manure (animal husbandry) is not taken into ac- count. Indeed, poultry is bred for its meat or eggs, not for its manure. Moreover, the impact of animal husbandry is identical for all of the compared treatment scenarios.

Combustion of poultry manure has a low environmental impact compared to land spreading, which is considered the most com- mon treatment option for manure. The recovery of energy to pro- duce electricity strongly reduces the impact of combustion in the impact categories climate change and fossil depletion, even when the produced electricity substitutes electricity from natural gas combustion. In the same worst case scenario (i.e. substitution of electricity from natural gas) for combustion, the production of mineral N fertilizer, to replace the N from the combusted poultry manure, did not increase the environmental impact significantly compared to land spreading. Indeed, the large atmospheric emis- sions of NH3, NOx and N20 that occur when manure is spread on agricultural land, cause a high environmental impact in the cate- gories terrestrial acidification, particulate matter formation, marine eutrophication and climate change.

The physical and chemical properties of poultry manure allow direct combustion in a fluidized bed combustor. However, for other manure types, e.g. pig and dairy cattle manure, pre-treatment is necessary, or other treatment techniques can be used. To evaluate their environmental performance, the key parameters are NH3, NOx and N20 emissions, as well as substitution of fossil energy (De Vries et al., 2012), similarly to the evaluation for poultry manure in this paper.

39: The environmental impact of two treatment options for poultry manure was compared; combustion with subsequent recycling of the ash and direct land spreading of the manure. In contrast to previous research of e.g. Reijnders and Huijbregts (2005), a zero-

Fig. 4. Environmental impact normalized for EU25+3 in 11 impact categories, for direct land spreading of poultry manure and for combustion of poultry manure according to two scenarios: (i) with replacement of electricity from gas, including production of NH4NO3, and (ii) with replacement of electricity from coal.
